coredata.dc:title: Sequential determination of cerium (IV) ion and ascorbic acid via a novel organic framework: A subtle interplay between intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and aggregated-induced-emission (AIE)
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiangqian
Lai, Zhijun
Gu, Jiapei
Liu, Wanqiang
Gao, Jinwei
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: Construction of systems with variable electron distribution is a relatively new method in design of responsive fluorophores. Herein, 4,4′,4″,4‴-(ethene-1,1,2,2-trayltetrakis (benzene-4,1-diyl))tetrakis(1-carboxymethyl-pyridin-1-ium)bromide (LG556) is developed and its specific spectral behavior has been verified based on perturbation involving intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) via the connected electron donor and acceptor. In the presence of Ce4+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of LG556 can be efficiently quenched due to the disassembly of LG556 nanoaggregates and inner filter effect (IFE). The sensitivity is dependent on the concentration of Ce4+ with an excellent linear range 1–150 μM and a low detection limit of 0.62 μM. At this stage, the working principle behind the aggregated-induced-emission (AIE) has been employed and the difficulty lying in aggregate formation is overcome. The incorporated ascorbic acid causes the reduction of Ce4+ to Ce3+ and the re-assembly of LG556 has been realized. The detection limit has been evaluated to be 39.2 nM, which is lower than most of the reported results. Therefore, the “on-off-on” signal conversion leads to a reversible IMPLICATION logic gate and the molecular probe has been applied for the analysis of Ce4+ and ascorbic acid in pure aqueous solution with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intramolecular charge transfer
Sensor
Aggregation induced emission (AIE)
Logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthetic and characterizations of probe LG556
Detection Ce4+ ions in aqueous solutions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of probe LG556 (10 μM) aqueous solution in the presence of different concentrations of Ce4+ (0–300 μM) (Inset: Photos of probe LG556 aqueous solution without (left) and with (right) 300 μM Ce4+ ions under the excitation of 365 nm UV light).
Fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F changes of probe LG556 (10 μM) aqueous solution at 556 nm in the presence of different concentrations of Ce4+ (1–150 μM).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of probe LG556 (10 μM) aqueous solution in the presence of 300 μM Ce4+ or different interference analytes (Inset: Photos of probe LG556 aqueous solution in the presence of 300 μM Ce4+ or different interference analytes under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, a blank, b K+, c Na+, d Co2+, e Cu2+, f Zn2+, g Mg2+, h Mn2+, I Hg+, j Ni2+, k Fe3+, l Al3+, m Ce3+, n NO3 −, o Cl−, p HS−, q SO4 2−, r SO3 2−, s CO3 2−, t Ce4+). (B) Fluorescence intensity of probe LG556 (10 μM) aqueous solution in the presence of 300 μM Ce4+ or different interference analytes.

Fluorescence spectra of LG556-Ce
4+ (10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+) aqueous solution in the presence of different concentrations of AA (0–50 μM).

Fluorescence intensity changes of LG556-Ce
4+ (10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+) aqueous solution at 556 nm in the presence of different concentrations of AA (0–20 μM).

Fluorescence spectra of probe LG556-Ce
4+ (10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+) aqueous solution in the presence of 50 μM AA or 200 μM different interference analytes (Inset: Photos of probe LG556-Ce
4+ (10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+) aqueous solution in the presence of 50 μM AA or 200 μM different interference analytes under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, 1 blank, 2 Ala, 3 Gly, 4 Lys, 5 Asn, 6 Cys 7 Arg, 8 Asp, 9 His, 10 Trp, 11 Leu, 12 Al3+, 13 Fe3+, 14 Zn2+, 15 Mn2+, 16 Mg2+, 17 Cl−, 18 SO4 2−, 19 NO3 −, 20 AA).

(A) Fluorescence spectra showing reversibility of probe LG556 aqueous solution upon treating with Ce4+ and AA; blue line: LG556 (10 μM), red line: LG556-Ce
4+ (10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+), green line: LG556-Ce
4+
-AA(10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+ + 50 μM AA), purple line, LG556-Ce
4+
-AA-Ce
4+ (10 μM LG556 + 300 μM Ce4+ + 50 μM AA + 300 μM Ce4+); (B) Reversible cyclic changes of probe LG556 upon treating with Ce4+ and AA alternatively.

(A) UV–vis spectra of LG556 (10 μM) and LG556 (10 μM) + Ce4+ (100 μM) in aqueous solution. (B) UV–vis absorbance changes of various concentration Ce4+ (0–100 μM) addition into LG556 (10 μM) aqueous solution.
TEM images of (A) LG556, (B) LG556-Ce4+, (C) LG556-Ce4+-AA and SEM images of (D) LG556-Ce4+-AA.

(A) “ON-OFF” schematic representation for LG556 in the presence of Ce4+ and AA, (B) logic symbol, (C) Truth table of IMPLICATION logic gate, (D) Output signals of logic gate (λem = 556 nm) in the presence of inputs Ce4+ and AA.
Schematic illustration of LG556 based fluorescence probe for sequential detection of Ce4+ and AA.

head.ce:title: Sequential determination of cerium (IV) ion and ascorbic acid via a novel organic framework: A subtle interplay between intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and aggregated-induced-emission (AIE)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan 411201, China

Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Materials, South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Materials, South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of systems with variable electron distribution is a relatively new method in design of responsive fluorophores. Herein, 4,4′,4″,4‴-(ethene-1,1,2,2-trayltetrakis (benzene-4,1-diyl))tetrakis(1-carboxymethyl-pyridin-1-ium)bromide (LG556) is developed and its specific spectral behavior has been verified based on perturbation involving intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) via the connected electron donor and acceptor. In the presence of Ce4+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of LG556 can be efficiently quenched due to the disassembly of LG556 nanoaggregates and inner filter effect (IFE). The sensitivity is dependent on the concentration of Ce4+ with an excellent linear range 1–150 μM and a low detection limit of 0.62 μM. At this stage, the working principle behind the aggregated-induced-emission (AIE) has been employed and the difficulty lying in aggregate formation is overcome. The incorporated ascorbic acid causes the reduction of Ce4+ to Ce3+ and the re-assembly of LG556 has been realized. The detection limit has been evaluated to be 39.2 nM, which is lower than most of the reported results. Therefore, the “on-off-on” signal conversion leads to a reversible IMPLICATION logic gate and the molecular probe has been applied for the analysis of Ce4+ and ascorbic acid in pure aqueous solution with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Electron distribution within organic chromophores via intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) between donor and acceptor is one of the most frequently discussed topics in photoreaction [1–6]. Generally speaking, the typical feature in ICT frameworks will be derived from dual absorption signals: the short wavelength peak is caused by the conjugated rings and long wavelength one has been related to the electron re-distribution from donor to acceptor. These two forms coexist in the ground state and it can result in further changes of ionization in the excited states. Variable spectral effects will be developed due to the presence of a variety of physicochemical parameters and the fluorescence of molecular scaffold has been reflected by the changes of emission intensity or peak wavelength shifts [7–10]. Although the formation of ICT states can occur in single molecular level, many of the common luminophores will suffer from the detrimental effect of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in the condensed phase. The emergence of AIE fluorophores (AIEgens) offers a straightforward way to thoroughly overcome the ACQ problem [11–18]. Up to date, a number of organic molecules have been found to show a pronounced AIE effect, such as hexaphenylsilole (the first-discovered AIEgen), tetraphenylethene (TPE), distyrylanthracene (DSA), tetraphenylpyrazine (TPP), quinoline-malononitrile (QM), 2-phenylcinnamylnitrile, cyano-substituted diarylethylene and aryl-substituted pyrrole derivatives [19–25]. TPE and its derivatives have accounted for more than half of all AIEgen compounds because of their advantages, for example, facile synthesis and easy functionalization, good stability and tunable emission. It can be accepted that the traditional low molecular weight molecules will easily accumulate onto the surfaces of biological tissues and the unexpected concentration quenching effect caused by formation of aggregates would be a main obstacle for the exploration of biosensors employed in real application. Since the concept of aggregation induced emission (AIE) was discovered by Tang's group in 2001, AIE would be a photophysical feature concerning the light emission in the form of highly concentrated or solid state. It provides a platform for us to utilize emissions derived from aggregates and establish new structure-property relationships. It has gained tremendous interest in optoelectronic devices, stimuli responsive materials, wash-free cell imaging, chemotherapy, chemosensors and biosensors because of its outstanding fluorescence property, high emission efficiency and larger signal-to-noise ratio with minimized background fluorescence [26–33].

Cerium (Ce) is the most abundant lanthanide elements found in the Earth's crust. It is widely used in luminescence, chemical engineering, agriculture, household, therapeutic application and so on [34–37]. Ce (IV) ion has long been used in combination with sulfate for the chemiluminescence detection of various inorganic and organic substances. Recent study demonstrated that Ce (IV) ions and its complexes have been employed to cleave phosphate ester bonds and amide linkages [38–40]. However, abnormal levels of Ce4+ ions can lead to many diseases such as lung embolisms, skin lesions, itching or cardiovascular disorders [41].

Ascorbic acid (AA), also called vitamin C (VC), is a vital compound in human diet. It plays an important role in many biochemical processes (collagen synthesis, enzymatic reactions, scurvy prevention, enhance the resistance and immunity of the human body) in the human body and thus is essential for human health [42–44]. Moreover, AA has a positive effect on the prevention and treatment of scurvy, common cold, mental illness, cancer and AIDS. As one of the important biological molecules, it is widely used as an antioxidant in beverages, food, pharmaceutical formulations and animal feed [45–49]. However, the literatures report that inadequate intake of AA is related to many diseases, such as disturbances in the collagen metabolism, gingival bleeding, scurvy, cardiovascular disease and cancer. Meanwhile, excessive amounts of AA will cause urinary oxalate calculus, diarrhea, stomach cramps, affect embryonic development and reduce the fertility of women. It can be valuable to measure AA distribution in the blood so as to suppress the risks of human body [50–53]. Considering the important roles of Ce4+ ions and AA played in our daily life, it is valuable to develop a facile, rapid, economical method for the onsite monitoring Ce4+ ions and AA.

The molecule developed to undergo ICT process should meet the basic criteria and it contains electron-donor in the vicinity of electron-acceptor, which will intensify the charge transfer effect due to excitation. However, the determination of its monomeric fluorescence would be inhibited by varying the polarity of the medium. In the current study, the incorporation of higher percentage of water (>70%) has caused partial reduction in emission intensity and the potential concentration-quenching effect has been a large obstacle to the fabrication of fluorescence sensory systems. Accordingly, the structural rigidity was adjusted by the introduction of tetraphenylethene units and high rotation barrier was circumvented. In detail, we have designed and synthesized a novel fluorescent probe LG556 for the detection of two targets in pure aqueous medium with high selectivity and high sensitivity (Scheme 1 ). LG556 showed a remarkable “switch off” response toward Ce4+ based on complexation and disaggregation. The detection limit for Ce4+ using LG556 can be as low as 0.62 μM with an excellent linear range of 1–150 μM. The employment of AA to the solution will change the valence of cerium ions and it can easily recover the fluorescence of the system by taking advantage of luminogenic aggregation and the re-assembly of probe LG556. The limit of detection (LOD) for AA was calculated to be 39.2 nM. In addition, a molecular logic gate with the IMPLICATION function based on these unique sensing characteristics was established. Ce4+ and AA were assigned as two input signals and fluorescence intensity at 556 nm was used as an output signal.

1,1,2,2-Tetrakis(4-(pyridin-4-yl)phenyl)ethane (99%), 3-bromopropanoic acid (95%), Ascorbic acid (AA, 99.99%), L-cysteine (Cys, 99%), L(+)-Arginine (Arg, 99%), L-Aspartic Acid (Asp, 98%), L-Histidine (His, 99%), L-Tryptophan (Trp, 99%), L-Leucine (Leu, 99%), L-Asparagine (Asn, 99%), l-Lysine (Lys, 98.5%), Glycine (Gly, 99%), L-Alanine, (Ala, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Acetonitrile (CH3CN), Dichloromethane (DCM), Petroleum ether (PE), Ethanol (EtOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Methanol (MeOH), Tetrahydrofuran (THF), Dimethyl sulfone (DMSO), Ethyl acetate (EA), Acetone and metal salts such as Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6 (99%), Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (99.95%), ZnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, MnCl2, Zn(NO)3, Al(NO3)3, NaHSO3, Co(NO3)2, MgSO4, CuCl2, FeCl3, PbCO3, AgNO3, CdSO4, NaHS, NaHSO3, NaOH were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification. The distilled water used in all experiments possessed a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ/cm.

1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance 400 MHz NMR Spectrometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 450 W xenon lamp as a light source (Hitachi, Japan). UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a TECHCOMP spectrophotometer in the range of 200–600 nm with a slit of 2 nm (TECHCOMP, Shanghai, China). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images was measured using a Hitachi S-3500 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic and characterizations of probe LG556
ce:section.ce:para: 1,1,2,2-Tetrakis(4-(pyridin-4-yl)phenyl)ethene (200 mg, 0.32 mmol) and 3-bromopropanoic acid (117 mg, 0.77 mmol) were added in the solution of 10 mL CH3CN and 10 mL CH2Cl2. Then, the mixture was refluxed at 120 °C for 3 days under nitrogen atmosphere. The reaction mixture was concentrated by rotary evaporation and washed by using petroleum ether and acetone for three times. A bright yellow powder was obtained by filtration, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h (145 mg, 91%). The synthesis route and structure of LG556 were shown in Fig. S1. 1H NMR (400 MHz, D2O) δ (ppm) 8.68–8.67 (d, 8H), 8.17 (d, 8H), 7.73 (d, 8H), 7.39 (d, 8H), 4.30 (t, 8H) (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Ce4+ ions in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of probe LG556 (10 mM) and various interference analytes (10 mM) were prepared in distilled water. For Ce4+ ions detection, different amount of Ce4+ ions were added in a series of 1.0 mL 10 μM LG556 aqueous solution. The fluorescence spectra were recorded between 450 and 780 nm wavelength range at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm, and excitation and emission slit widths were 5.0 nm and 5.0 nm, respectively. All the experiments were measured at room temperature.

For AA detection, different concentrations of AA were added in a series of 1.0 mL 10 μM LG556 aqueous solution containing 300 μM Ce4+ ions. The fluorescence spectra were recorded between 450 and 780 nm wavelength range at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm, and excitation and emission slit widths were 5.0 nm and 5.0 nm, respectively. All the experiments were measured at room temperature.

The D-π-A type framework has been demonstrated to be a potential candidate for exploring optical activity. In such molecule design, tetraphenylethene moiety possessed effective electron donating capability and the pyridine units with carboxyl groups would show strong affinity to electrons. Therefore, the donor-acceptor pairs to induce intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) were established in fluorophore. When external factors were considered, solvent effect was generally the first topic to correlate with spectral properties. The interpretation of solvent-dependent emission spectra was given in Fig. S3. It was observed that the broad band was insensitive to less polar solvents such as ethyl acetate, THF and dichloromethane. In contrast, the emission curves were sharply reduced upon the exposure to the polar environments. Since the electron-donating and accepting groups were incorporated into the molecular structure, the improvement in charge separation might be achieved in polar solvents. Additionally, the spectra change observed in the presence of ethanol or DMF would be caused by hydrogen bonding of hydroxyl/carbonyl to pyridine or carboxyl units. Such specific solvent-fluorophore interactions would affect the excited state energy.

We also measured the fluorescence spectra of LG556 in DMSO-water mixtures with different water proportions. As shown in Fig. S4, LG556 gave rise to a strong emission band at 544 nm in pure DMSO that originated from the ICT property. Upon increasing the fraction of water (0–60%), the emission intensity could be maintained relatively stable and such results revealed that the extension of aromatic or heterocyclic rings would emit more efficiently in aqueous mixture. Although the higher concentration of water would result in slight reduction in emission intensity, its quantum yield could still be determined as 6.1% in 100% water medium. Moreover, its emission peak evolved from 544 nm to yellow region at 556 nm, indicating that the large orientation polarizability of water would significantly affect the energy difference between the ground and excited states. Considering the practical performance in aqueous solution, pure water was finally used as the solvent for the following spectroscopy experiments.

The acid-base property is normally considered to be the key point in view of real-world applications. Aiming at applying LG556 in different physiological or industrial fields, the effects of pH in the analyte solution on the fluorescence were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the response was measured at the same circumstances with the exception of pH values, revealing that the fluorescence intensity of LG556 was almost constant within a broad pH range of 1–14 and the pH change had a negligible effect on the optical signals. The results indicate that LG556 will possess enough stability under a wide variety of conditions.

Ce4+ ion has been regarded as one of the most important oxidants in lanthanide chemistry and the determination of such element will be very valuable in pharmaceutical formulation, fuel additive or electronics. To explore the sensitivity of the probe LG556 for such cation, various concentrations of Ce4+ ions were added into the solution and the corresponding fluorescence responses were recorded. As provided in Fig. 1 , the probe LG556 alone exhibited a strong emission peak at 556 nm. Upon the addition of various concentration of Ce4+ ions (0–300 μM), the fluorescence intensity were decreased gradually and quenched over 90% in the presence of 300 μM Ce4+ ions. The intensity ratio of F0/F (F0 and F were the fluorescence emission intensity of the probe LG556 without and with different concentrations Ce4+ ions, respectively) was proportional to the Ce4+ ions concentrations, which could be applied to quantitatively determine Ce4+ ions. As provided in Fig. 2 , an excellent linear relationship was afforded in the range of 1–150 μM. The detection limit of Ce4+ ions was calculated to be 0.62 μM according to the eq. DL = 3 × SD/slope, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 10). In order to evaluate the performance of determination, we have compared the detection limit for Ce4+ in this contribution with related literatures (Table S1). Although the detection limit was not the lowest, it still achieved an accepted value. Furthermore, in the following section, LG556 could exhibit a reversible manner due to the recognition of ascorbic acid. Additionally, the concept of logic gate could be established based on two analytes (Ce4+ and ascorbic acid).

Selectivity is a related parameter to evaluate the performance of the sensor. We measured the fluorescence intensity of probe LG556 in the presence of interference ions under the same conditions (Fig. 3 ). The experimental results showed that other ions have no effects on the photoluminescence intensities except that Ce4+ ions induced a significant fluorescence quenching response of probe LG556. The digital pictures captured for the interfering species were compared with cerium-incorporating sample, the selectivity observation for Ce4+ ions over other ions was remarkably high and the sharp switched-off change could be identified. All these results validated that present fluorescent sensing platform owned high specificity for the Ce4+ ions assay.

To develop an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for AA sensing, LG556 (10 μM) and Ce4+ (300 μM) aqueous solution were mixed prior to further experiment. The fluorescence spectra of LG556-Ce4+ systems in the presence of AA at different concentrations were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence of LG556-Ce4+ systems was obviously recovered with the increase of AA concentration ranging from 0 to 50 μM. Fig. 5 showed an excellent linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 (F and F0 were the fluorescence emission intensity of the LG556-Ce4+ systems in the presence and absence of various concentrations AA, respectively) and the concentration of AA in the range of 0–20 μM. The linear regression equation was Y = 0.4976 X + 0.5619 (R2 = 0.9947) and the detection limit was 39.2 nM based on the equation LOD = 3SD/Slop, where SD was the standard deviation of the blank signals of the LG556-Ce4+ systems.

To confirm the high selectivity of LG556-Ce4+ systems for the detection of AA, the fluorescence response of our probe toward other analytes, including 200 μM Ala, Gly, Lys, Asn, Cys, Arg, Asp, His, Trp, Leu, Al3+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2− and NO3 − were investigated. The overall fluorescence changes of LG556-Ce4+ systems were summarized in Fig. 6 . Only the color of LG556-Ce4+ system in the presence of AA gave a bright yellow emission, and there were almost no changes observed for other analytes. The significant difference in fluorescence was caused by the exposure to AA and other analytes would not interfere with the sensing process.

It is necessary to assess the reversibility of such system since numerous optical probes could be hardly re-generated due to the recognition of guest species which will substantially limit their practical applicability. As shown in Fig. 7A, further addition of Ce4+ ions to the LG556-Ce
4+
-AA aqueous solution, resulted in emission quench, thus confirming the molecular probe LG556 could be recovered again. This “ON-OFF” switching phenomenon could be repeated five times by alternate Ce4+ ions and AA additions (Fig. 7B).

To understand the proposed interaction mechanism, UV–vis titration spectra studies of probe LG556 + Ce4+ and probe LG556 + Ce4+ + AA were performed. As shown in Fig. 8A and B, two absorption peaks at 285 and 360 nm were observed in pure LG556 in aqueous solution. The absorption band at shorter wavelength was ascribed to the π-π* transition of the TPE chromophore, while the longer-wavelength absorption was attributed to charge transfer from the electron-donating TPE core to the peripheral electron-accepting pyridine group. Upon successive addition of Ce4+ ions, the absorbance peak at 360 nm increased gradually and hypsochromic shifted to 352 nm. In addition, the peak at 285 nm showed a significant enhancement. These results indicated that the carboxyl group on probe LG556 can efficiently coordinate with Ce4+ ions. The further incorporation of AA to LG556-Ce4+ systems would lead to the change in a reversible manner. The absorbance peak at 352 nm was suppressed gradually and red-shifted to 360 nm. Meanwhile, the absorbance signal at 285 nm was suppressed again (Fig. S6). These results would be closely related to the strong reduction ability of AA that could transform from Ce4+ to Ce3+. Moreover, inner filter effect (IFE) might be included. It is a crucial non-irradiation energy conversion model in spectrofluorometry, which usually occurs between the absorber and fluorophore arising from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by absorbers in the detection system [54,55]. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the excitation spectrum of LG556 was largely overlapped with the absorption peak of Ce4+. These results suggested that probe LG556 was developed for the determination of Ce4+ ions based on inner filter effect.

The shape and size of nanocrystals will in general determine its chemical reactivity and physical properties. Herein, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of probe LG556, LG556-Ce4+, LG556-Ce4+-AA and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of LG556-Ce4+-AA were explored to further confirm the sensing mechanism. As given in Fig. 9A, probe LG556 was homogeneously dispersed in the form of spherical nanoparticles with size of 166 nm. Upon the addition of Ce4+ ions, the diameter of the nanoparticles decreased sharply with an average diameter of 15.6 nm, which was attributed to cerium (IV)-induced disaggregation of probe LG556 (Fig. 9B). Interestingly, the formation of spherical nanoaggregate with a diameter of 155–180 nm was found after the encapsulation of AA to LG556-Ce4+ systems (Fig. 9C and D). These results supported that AA would possibly reduce Ce4+ to Ce3+ and lead to re-assembly of LG556 [39]. It has also been accepted that aggregation induced emission (AIE) is the typical phenomenon that will be non-emissive or very weakly emissive in disaggregation for tiny particles but become highly emissive in aggregated states due to the restricted intramolecular vibration [56–59]. In this contribution, the non-planar unit (tetraphenylethene) was introduced into the building block and the intramolecular rotation process was impeded. In this way, the non-radiative decay route has been suppressed and hence made LG556 emissive in water.

As demonstrated in Fig. 10 , the fluorescence switching behavior of probe LG556 in the presence of Ce4+ ions and AA can be employed as multiple molecular logic gates to describe the sensing procedure [60]. In the logic operations, probe LG556 serve as gates, while the addition of two species (Ce4+, input 1; AA, input 2) and fluorescence emission change at 556 nm are used as inputs and outputs, respectively. The threshold value has been taken as 0.5 of maximum output emission intensity at 556 nm and the relative emission intensity above this has been defined as “ON” (i.e., 1) state whereas, lower one as “OFF” (i.e., 0) states correspondingly. In IMPLICATION operations, the output is “0” only when input 1 is equal to “1”, and the input 2 has to be equal to “0”. In all the other three forms, the achieved output is “1”. Emission of LG556 allowed the design of IMPLICATION logic gate based on Boolean operations when Ce4+ ions and AA as inputs. For inputs, the presence of Ce4+ ions or AA is considered to be the “1” state and their absence as “0”. The working symbol of the IMPLICATION logic gate is provided in Fig. 10B. The truth table contains the details of all combinations and it is represented in Fig. 10C, showing that the input of Ce4+ ions and absence of AA (1/0), the fluorescence of probe LG556 is suppressed, and the output is “0”. In all other cases, the outputs are “1”.

The increasing exploitation of fluorescence has been realized by ICT molecules. A variety of analogs established on such design principle have been prepared in literatures, but the aggregation-caused quenching effect prevented the practical values due to the close vicinity in the condensed phase. To circumvent the main obstacle in optical sensing, tetraphenylethene moiety was introduced into 4,4′,4″,4‴-(ethene-1,1,2,2-trayltetrakis (benzene-4,1-diyl))tetrakis(1-carboxymethyl-pyridin-1-ium) bromide (LG556) and the AIE feature was exactly opposite to the quenching effect in water. The processes of LG556 responsive to Ce4+ and AA were verified by fluorescence spectrum titration, UV–vis spectrum titration, TEM and SEM. It was observed that LG556 undergo disaggregation in the presence of Ce4+ and along with a remarkable fluorescence quenching. The recovery of emission could be realized by the addition of AA and lead to re-assembly of LG556. This “on-off-on” fluorescence mode can be used for the sequential detection of Ce4+ and AA. Additionally, it was acted as the function of a sequential logic circuit at molecular level by using the above fluorescence changes.

Xiangqian Li: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhijun Lai: Investigation, Methodology. Jiapei Gu: Software. Wanqiang Liu: Formal analysis. Jinwei Gao: Validation. Qianming Wang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J. W. thanks grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC)-Guangdong Joint funding support (No. U1801256) and Innovation team project by the Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009). X.Q. thanks for the support of Innovation Project of Graduate School of South China Normal University (2019LKXM007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.112705.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amorphous pure organic polymers for heavy-atom-free efficient room-temperature phosphorescence emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ma, C. Xu, J. Wang and H. Tian, Amorphous pure organic polymers for heavy-atom-free efficient room-temperature phosphorescence emission, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2018, 57, 10854-10858.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intramolecular charge transfer controls switching between room temperature phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence
